record_office_terminology.land_records: Land and Titles Division, High Court of American Samoa (communal land records under Samoan custom; matai title successions; aiga land disputes -- approximately 90% of land is communal, not individually owned)
record_office_terminology.probate: High Court of American Samoa (limited western-style probate; most property passes through Samoan custom via aiga/matai system rather than individual wills)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Territorial Registrar, American Samoa Government (civil marriage registration under territorial law; many marriages also recorded in church registers)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1900 (US Navy administration began recording some vital events; systematic civil registration developed gradually under territorial government; church records predate civil registration by decades)

burned_county_authority: American Samoa has no burned districts. Record losses are attributable to tropical climate deterioration, hurricanes (notably the 1889 Apia hurricane, Cyclone Val 1991, and Cyclone Ofa 1990), and the limited development of western-style record-keeping in a Polynesian oral culture society. The American Samoa Historic Preservation Office (ASHPO) in Pago Pago is the primary cultural and historical repository.
administrative_notes: American Samoa is an unincorporated, unorganized US territory in the South Pacific, consisting of five main islands (Tutuila, Aunu'u, Ta'u, Ofu, Olosega) plus Rose Atoll and Swains Island. The territory was ceded to the United States through treaties with Samoan paramount chiefs in 1900 (Tutuila) and 1904 (Manu'a). Swains Island was annexed by Act of Congress in 1925. The US Navy administered the territory from 1900 to 1951, when authority transferred to the US Department of the Interior. American Samoa adopted its own constitution in 1967. Residents are US nationals (not citizens) unless individually naturalized. The territory is governed under Fa'a Samoa (the Samoan Way), a traditional system of communal governance through matai (chiefs) and village fono (councils). Approximately 90% of land is communal, held by aiga (extended families) under matai authority -- individual land ownership is constitutionally restricted. Genealogical research in American Samoa differs fundamentally from mainland US research: oral genealogies (gafa) maintained by matai and family elders are the primary source of family history, supplemented by church records (the oldest written records in the territory), US Navy administrative records at NARA, and the limited civil records of the territorial government. The matai system requires genealogical knowledge for title succession, making Samoan families highly aware of their lineage. FIPS recognizes 5 districts: Eastern, Manu'a, Rose Island (uninhabited), Swains Island, and Western.

core_repositories.description: The primary cultural and historical repository in American Samoa. ASHPO maintains archaeological site records, cultural resource surveys, oral history recordings, historical photographs, and documentation of traditional Samoan cultural practices. Essential for understanding the historical context of genealogical research in the territory. ASHPO also administers the National Register of Historic Places nominations for American Samoa.

core_repositories.source_name: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) -- American Samoa Records
core_repositories.repository: NARA, Washington, DC and College Park, MD
core_repositories.description: NARA holds the records of the US Naval Government of American Samoa (1900-1951) in Record Group 313 (Naval Operating Forces) and RG 126 (Office of Territories). These include censuses, vital event registers, correspondence, court records, land tenure documents, and administrative reports. RG 126 also contains records from the Department of the Interior's administration (1951-present). These are the most extensive government records for American Samoa's US period.

core_repositories.source_name: Feleti Barstow Public Library -- Pacific Islands Collection
core_repositories.repository: Feleti Barstow Public Library, Pago Pago, American Samoa
core_repositories.description: The territorial public library houses a Pacific Islands collection with local histories, government documents, newspapers (Samoa News), and reference materials on Samoan culture and genealogy. Limited but locally important collection for researchers in the territory.

familysearch_collections.description: FamilySearch has limited but growing collections for American Samoa. The LDS Church has a significant presence in the territory (missionaries arrived in 1888) and ward records supplement civil documentation. FamilySearch may hold indexed LDS ward records, some civil records, and Pacific Islands collections. The FamilySearch Wiki has research guidance for American Samoa.

Research guide for American Samoa genealogy covering available record types, repositories, Samoan naming conventions, and strategies for researching Polynesian families. Includes guidance on oral genealogy (gafa) and the matai system.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Samoa News, Pago Pago / Feleti Barstow Public Library
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: American Samoa's primary newspaper, published since 1969. Contains obituaries, marriage announcements, community news, court notices, and legal advertisements. Back issues available at the Feleti Barstow Public Library. Online archives for recent years. Essential for modern-era genealogical research in the territory.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: US Naval Government of American Samoa -- Official Publications and Reports
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: The US Naval Government published annual reports, newsletters, and administrative bulletins during its 1900-1951 administration. These contain population data, vital statistics, village reports, and community news. Held at NARA and the Library of Congress.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: American Samoa has no history of African slavery or African American populations in the context applicable to mainland US states. The territory is Polynesian with a distinct cultural history. This category is included for schema completeness. Note: Samoa had its own traditional forms of social hierarchy, but these are not comparable to chattel slavery in the Americas.

military_records.source_name: US Naval Station Tutuila and Military Records -- NARA
military_records.repository: NARA, Washington, DC and College Park, MD
military_records.description: The US Navy established a coaling station at Pago Pago in 1899 and administered the territory until 1951. NARA holds records of the naval station, the Fita Fita Guard (a Samoan unit of the US Navy, 1900-1952), and military operations in American Samoa during WWII (the territory was a significant staging area). Service records for Samoan sailors and Marines. The Fita Fita Guard records are particularly valuable for genealogical research as they document Samoan men who served in the US military.

military_records.source_name: WWII in American Samoa -- Military Records
military_records.repository: NARA / American Samoa Historic Preservation Office
military_records.description: During WWII, American Samoa was a significant US military base. Marines were stationed on Tutuila and the naval station was expanded. Records include military construction records, personnel files, and documentation of Samoan civilians employed by the military. ASHPO has documented WWII-era military sites in the territory.

land_and_property.source_name: Land and Titles Division -- High Court of American Samoa
land_and_property.repository: High Court of American Samoa, Fagatogo
land_and_property.description: The Land and Titles Division of the High Court is the central authority for communal land records and matai title successions in American Samoa. Approximately 90% of land is communal (held by aiga/extended families under matai authority). The court adjudicates disputes over land tenure and chiefly titles, producing records that document family relationships and genealogical claims. These records are the closest equivalent to mainland deed records but operate under Samoan customary law rather than western property law. Individual (freehold) land is very limited and constitutionally restricted.

vital_records.source_name: American Samoa Territorial Registrar -- Vital Records
vital_records.repository: Territorial Registrar, American Samoa Government, Pago Pago
vital_records.description: The Territorial Registrar maintains birth, death, and marriage records for American Samoa. Civil registration began under US Navy administration (1900) but early compliance was inconsistent. Modern vital records are maintained by the territorial government. Birth certificates, death certificates, and marriage licenses are available from the Registrar's office in Pago Pago. For events before systematic civil registration, church records are the primary alternative.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Congregational Christian Church of American Samoa (CCCAS) Records
church_and_cemetery.repository: CCCAS congregations throughout American Samoa / CCCAS headquarters, Pago Pago
church_and_cemetery.description: The CCCAS (descended from the London Missionary Society, est. 1830) is the largest and oldest Christian denomination in American Samoa. Individual congregations maintain baptism, marriage, and burial registers dating from the 1830s-1840s. These are the oldest written records in the territory and the single most important documentary source for Samoan genealogical research. Records are kept in Samoan language. Access requires contacting individual church pastors (faifeau).

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Catholic Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago -- Parish Records
church_and_cemetery.repository: Catholic parishes / Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago
church_and_cemetery.description: The Marist Fathers established the Catholic mission in Samoa in 1845. Parish records include baptisms, marriages, and burials. The Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago oversees Catholic parishes throughout the territory. Records in Samoan with some Latin, French, and English entries.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Village Cemeteries and Burial Practices -- American Samoa
church_and_cemetery.repository: American Samoa Historic Preservation Office / Individual village councils
church_and_cemetery.description: Samoan burial practices traditionally place graves on family land adjacent to homes. Village cemeteries and family burial sites are maintained by aiga. ASHPO has conducted cultural resource surveys documenting burial sites. Tombstone inscriptions (modern) and oral traditions about burial locations provide genealogical information.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: American Samoa Historic Preservation Office (ASHPO) -- Research Guidance
research_guides_and_societies.description: ASHPO provides guidance on researching American Samoa's cultural and historical resources, including traditional Samoan genealogy (gafa), the matai system, and available documentary sources. ASHPO staff can assist researchers navigating the territory's unique record landscape.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Pacific Islands Studies Resources -- University of Hawaii at Manoa
research_guides_and_societies.repository: Hamilton Library, University of Hawaii at Manoa, Honolulu
research_guides_and_societies.description: The University of Hawaii's Pacific Islands collection is one of the largest academic collections on the Pacific region. Holds published histories, ethnographies, and government documents for American Samoa. The Pacific Islands Development Program and East-West Center also have relevant materials.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Bernice Pauahi Bishop Museum -- Pacific Islands Collections
research_guides_and_societies.description: The Bishop Museum holds one of the world's largest collections of Polynesian and Pacific Islands cultural artifacts, manuscripts, and archival materials. The museum's archives include ethnographic fieldwork records, photographs, and genealogical materials related to Samoan culture.

historical_counties_extinct.description: American Samoa has no extinct districts. The current five-district structure (Eastern, Manu'a, Rose Island, Swains Island, Western) reflects the FIPS classification of the territory. Prior to US administration (pre-1900), Samoa was organized by traditional village polities and paramount chieftainships (Tui Manu'a, Tui Atua, etc.) rather than western-style administrative districts. The district boundaries approximate the traditional divisions of Tutuila (Eastern and Western) and the Manu'a island group, with Rose Atoll and Swains Island as separate administrative entities. No districts have been created or abolished since the territory was organized.

burned_counties_summary.notes: American Samoa has no burned districts. Record losses are attributable to the territory's tropical climate (humidity, insects, mold), destructive hurricanes (the 1889 Apia hurricane, Cyclone Ofa 1990, Cyclone Val 1991, the 2009 tsunami), and the fundamentally oral nature of traditional Samoan record-keeping. Western-style civil records began only in 1900 with US Navy administration and remain limited compared to mainland US states. Church records (from the 1830s) are the oldest written records in the territory and have suffered variable losses. The small population and limited institutional infrastructure mean that archival preservation resources are constrained.
